narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile like your solid LSAT score and full-time commitment to studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 77% chance that you would not pass, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection to help ensure the program's success rates. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower rankings in school deciles—specifically, your Year 3 performance placed you in the bottom decile compared to peers, which had the strongest pull toward forecasting a bar exam struggle. Your Year 1 decile was also relatively low, adding to that concern, while your middle-family income bracket contributed somewhat to the risk assessment, as it aligns with patterns seen in students who faced hurdles. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36 was close to the typical student's 36.7 and actively worked in your favor, pushing the prediction toward success, much like your choice to study full-time, which is the norm and helped balance things slightly. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 fell below the average of 3.21, playing a minor role in the overall concern. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your academic deciles stand out as notably lower, which weighed more heavily than your other solid attributes in this model's eyes. This isn't a reflection of your potential or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers—many with similar backgrounds overcome these metrics through targeted growth. To move forward, we recommend focusing on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study groups tailored to law prep. Retaking the LSAT if feasible could strengthen your case even more, and exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique story might open new doors. You're capable of turning this around—keep pushing, and consider reapplying after addressing these key areas. We're rooting for your success.
